title: Older Australians on low incomes are at risk of being pushed out of private healthcare
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 WTalkingPoint Older Australians on low incomes are at risk of being pushed out of private healthcare Hobart Mercury Dr Rachel David 2 May 2026 726 words English MRCURY 40 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Scrapping the higher health insurance rebate for seniors is a false economy, that is neither fair nor smart, writes Dr Rachel David For decades, hundreds of thousands of older Australians on modest incomes did exactly what successive governments encouraged them to do – take out private health insurance and contribute to their own healthcare. They kept paying through their working life and retirement, absorbing rising premiums and living costs because health cover gave them certainty, choice and timely access to care. Now Australians aged 65 and above are being told the rules are about to change. Under an Albanese government proposal, they could face hundreds of dollars in additional annual costs for their health insurance – or risk losing the cover they have maintained for decades. That is neither fair nor smart policy. Many Australians over 65 live on limited incomes, including age pensioners earning less than $30,000 a year. A significant proportion live in rural and regional communities where access to timely public healthcare can already be difficult. They devote a substantial share of their income to healthcare precisely because they want to remain independent and avoid long waits for care at this time of life. The government’s proposal to reduce private health insurance rebates for people over 65, bringing their rebates into line with younger people, may sound equitable on paper. In practice, it is a blunt measure that risks pushing vulnerable Australians out of health cover right when they need it most. About 39 per cent of people with health insurance earn less than $55,000 annually. Among them are more than 900,000 older Australians who will be affected by these changes. Government modelling suggests about 44,000 older Australians will drop their insurance by 2027-28 as result of this policy. What this does not capture is the far larger number likely to downgrade their insurance, leaving them with insufficient cover for care such as joint replacements and cancer treatment. Those most likely to leave or downgrade are also those with the greatest health needs. When older patients exit the private system, costs do not disappear. They shift directly onto already stretched public hospitals, emergency departments and elective surgery waiting lists. Policies that appear to save money in one part of the health budget can quickly create higher costs elsewhere. Indeed, a 2023 Finity report commissioned by the Department of Health and Aged Care found the private health insurance rebate delivers net savings to governments because reductions in public hospital demand outweigh the cost of the subsidy itself. In 2023, University of Newcastle researchers found that on average, the Australian government saves $554 for each person it helps with its health insurance subsidies per year. Health insurers are not arguing reform should never occur. Nor should higher-income retirees receive the same level of taxpayer support as Australians doing it tough. Reducing rebates for retirees on six-figure incomes is reasonable. But reform should be targeted. A better approach would retain higher rebate support for Australians over 65 earning under $55,000, including pensioners, so rebate support reflects capacity to pay. The government could also strengthen participation in health insurance by modestly increasing the Medicare Levy Surcharge for high-income earners who currently choose to remain uninsured despite having the means to contribute more to their healthcare costs. More than half a million higher-income Australians currently pay the surcharge rather than hold insurance, weakening risk pools and placing upward pressure on premiums for 55 per cent of Australians paying for cover. That is where genuine structural reform lies. Older Australians on low incomes feel they upheld their side of the bargain. They planned ahead, stayed insured and contributed to a mixed public-private health system designed to share demand across both sectors. Asking them now to absorb steep price rises for health cover and private care risks breaking that promise. Budget repair matters. But good policy reform protects vulnerable Australians while strengthening the system as a whole. Australia can build a sustainable health system without pricing older Australians out of the cover they rely on – and without shifting costs onto public hospitals already under strain. Dr Rachel David is chief executive of Private Healthcare Australia Hobart Mercury-20260502-Hobart-40-000500600722 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: Older Australians on low incomes may face significant increases in private health insurance costs due to proposed government changes to health insurance rebates. This could lead to many downgrading or dropping their coverage, potentially shifting costs to the public healthcare system. The proposal has raised concerns about fairness and the impact on vulnerable populations who have relied on private health insurance for access to timely care.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: Proposed reduction in private health insurance rebates for Australians over 65 could increase their costs significantly.
Approximately 44,000 older Australians may drop their insurance by 2027-28, with many more likely to downgrade their coverage.
The changes could exacerbate pressures on public hospitals and healthcare services.
relevance: Understanding the implications of health policy changes is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals involved in health insurance and retirement planning.
